Document:
# AN/PRC-117G Radio
The AN/PRC-117G is a manpack radio used by trained radio operators. It can store several nets, transmit over long distances, and relay nearby friendly headset traffic while powered.
## Quick Setup
Before leaving the staging area:
- Wear the radio in your [bold]back slot[/bold]. - Alt-click the radio or open its verb menu. - Select [bold]Open Radio Panel[/bold]. - Select [bold]+ ADD NET[/bold]. - Give the slot a short label, such as [bold]CMD[/bold]. - Select [bold]TUNE[/bold]. - Choose a named net from the [bold]NET[/bold] tab, or enter a direct frequency. - Select the slot row to make it active. - Insert a matching COMSEC fill card when required. - Check the callsign shown on the radio panel.
Transmit through the active slot with:
[color=#a4885c]:r Message here.[/color]
A worn radio normally transmits using the wearer's assigned callsign. If no callsign is assigned, it transmits as [bold]UNKNOWN STATION[/bold].
## Operator Access
Only trained operators can open the radio panel and transmit directly through the radio.
A worn radio relays traffic only when it is carried by a trained operator. A deployed retransmission station continues relaying without a wearer.
### GOVFOR trained roles
- Radio Traffic Operator - Commander - Officer Adjutant - Advisor - Platoon Sergeant
### CLF trained roles
- Cell Leader
Nearby headset users do not need radio training. They only need to be within range of a powered radio carrying their net.
## Handset
Any nearby person can use the radio's handset without radio training.
Alt-click the [bold]wearer[/bold], or a deployed radio, and select [bold]Take Handset[/bold]. The handset transmits over the radio's active slot with [color=#a4885c]:r[/color].
The handset:
- Reaches approximately two tiles. - Disconnects when the user moves out of range. - Uses the handset user's own callsign. - Can be used by one person at a time.
Alt-click again to return the handset.
A squad leader can use an attached RTO's handset to reach a command net that is not available through the squad leader's headset.
## Slots
The radio can store up to [bold]four[/bold] slots.
Each slot can contain a named net or a direct frequency. Slot labels are only shown on that radio and do not change the selected net.
Select a slot row to make it active. [color=#a4885c]:r[/color] always transmits through the active slot.
[bold]Control[/bold]
[bold]Function[/bold]
[bold]Details[/bold]
[bold]NET[/bold]
Select a named faction net.
Used for normal net setup.
[bold]FREQ[/bold]
Enter a direct frequency.
Can also be used to monitor enemy frequencies.
[bold]CLEAR[/bold]
Remove the selected net or frequency.
Keeps the slot and its label.
[bold]DEL[/bold]
Delete the slot.
Another slot becomes active when necessary.
Frequencies can be entered with or without a decimal point.
[color=#a4885c]1606[/color] and [color=#a4885c]1.606[/color] select the same frequency.
## Callsigns
Faction radio traffic is identified by callsign.
The sender's character name and role are not transmitted over headsets or manpack radios. Friendly and hostile listeners see the sender's callsign instead.
Face-to-face speech is unaffected.
Faction members receive callsigns automatically when they deploy.
[bold]Example[/bold]
[bold]Station[/bold]
HAVOC 6
Commanding officer
HAVOC 5
Executive officer or second-in-command
HAVOC 7
Senior non-commissioned officer
HAVOC ROMEO
Command radio operator
HAVOC OPS
Staff officers
ALPHA ACTUAL
Alpha squad leader
ALPHA ROMEO
Radio operator attached to Alpha
ALPHA 1-1 ... 1-N
Alpha squad members
[bold]ACTUAL[/bold] identifies the person in charge speaking personally rather than an operator transmitting on their behalf.
Squad leaders answer as their element's ACTUAL station, for example [bold]ALPHA ACTUAL[/bold]. At platoon level, [bold]HAVOC 6 ACTUAL[/bold] means the commander has taken the handset personally instead of the RTO.
### Comms Net Directory
The [bold]Comms Net Directory[/bold] shows which person is assigned to each callsign. It is available on command-post laptops and overwatch consoles.
Authorized radio personnel can:
- Rename element words. - Reassign individual suffixes. - Review current callsign assignments.
A worn AN/PRC normally follows the wearer's assigned callsign. The panel displays this as [bold]AUTO[/bold].
The radio updates automatically when:
- It is given to another person. - The wearer's callsign is changed. - The wearer changes squads.
### Callsign override
A fixed station callsign can be set from the radio panel.
- Select a callsign from the [bold]PRESETS[/bold] menu, or enter one manually. - Manual callsigns can contain up to 16 characters. - Select [bold]SET[/bold] to apply the override. - Clear the field and select [bold]SET[/bold] to remove it.
Fixed callsigns can be used for command posts, retransmission sites, or other permanent stations.
For message format, prowords, and examples, see [textlink="Voice Procedure" link="AU14CommsVoiceProcedure"].
## Direct Frequencies
A slot containing a frequency without a named net is a direct-frequency slot.
A [textlink="tuned headset" link="AU14CommsHeadsets"] can communicate on the same frequency with [color=#a4885c]:x[/color].
Without a relay:
- Traffic is clear within [bold]30 tiles[/bold]. - Traffic may be lightly degraded up to [bold]50 tiles[/bold]. - Traffic is not received beyond [bold]50 tiles[/bold].
With a powered AN/PRC carrying the frequency:
- The radio relays traffic between nearby users. - Both users must be within [bold]50 tiles[/bold] of the radio.
Use [color=#a4885c]:r[/color] when the direct-frequency slot is active on the radio.
Use [color=#a4885c]:x[/color] from a tuned headset.
## Operating Modes
Select [bold]MODE[/bold] to cycle the radio's operating mode.
[bold]Mode[/bold]
[bold]Purpose[/bold]
[bold]Benefits[/bold]
[bold]Limitations[/bold]
[bold]FH[/bold]
Frequency-hopping traffic.
Reduces jamming by one tier.
Uses 1.5× battery and creates a small direction-finding signature.
[bold]SC[/bold]
Normal secured traffic.
Efficient. Secured traffic creates no direction-finding signature.
Provides no jamming resistance.
[bold]CT[/bold]
Cipher traffic between AN/PRC radios.
Hidden and unreadable without matching fill. No direction-finding signature.
Requires fill. Standard headsets cannot receive it.
[bold]PT[/bold]
Plain-text broadcast.
Uses 0.5× battery.
Readable by any compatible receiver and creates the largest direction-finding risk.
## Jamming
FH reduces jamming by one tier.
- Heavy jamming becomes medium. - Medium jamming becomes light. - Light jamming is ignored.
Unlike a normal headset, the AN/PRC can transmit while inside a jammer field. The message may be degraded instead of being blocked completely.
## MON, SCAN, and SQL
### MON
[bold]MON[/bold] enables receive-only monitoring.
While MON is enabled:
- The radio receives traffic on its loaded nets. - [color=#a4885c]:r[/color] transmission is blocked. - Headset channel keys remain usable.
### SCAN
[bold]SCAN[/bold] listens to all loaded nets.
When traffic is received, the radio changes its active slot to the slot that received the message. It does not automatically return to the previous slot.
### SQL
[bold]SQL[/bold] controls squelch. Squelch filters degraded signals and static, but does not block clear traffic.
[bold]SQL[/bold]
[bold]Filtering[/bold]
[bold]0[/bold]
Passes all traffic, including heavy static.
[bold]1[/bold]
Blocks heavy static.
[bold]2[/bold]
Blocks medium jammer garble.
[bold]3[/bold]
Default. Blocks normal jammer garble.
[bold]4[/bold]
Maximum filtering. Weak relay traffic may also be suppressed.
Lowering SQL allows more degraded traffic to pass through.
## COMSEC Fill
COMSEC fill is stored on a physical card inserted into the radio.
The radio accepts one fill card at a time. The COMSEC panel displays [bold]SECURED[/bold] when the loaded card matches the traffic being used.
Faction headsets contain their own net keys. The AN/PRC requires a matching fill card to participate securely.
Transmitting on a secured faction net without matching fill:
- Displays a warning. - Still sends the message. - Sends the message in the clear.
A radio can accept another faction's fill card. A current captured card allows compatible secured traffic from that faction to be read.
## ZEROIZE, DESTROY, and RECRYPTO
### ZEROIZE
[bold]ZEROIZE[/bold] ejects the loaded fill card.
The card is placed in a free hand. If no hand is available, it is dropped nearby.
### DESTROY
[bold]DESTROY[/bold] permanently removes the loaded fill card.
### RECRYPTO
[bold]RECRYPTO[/bold] advances the active fill generation for the card's faction.
Older cards from that faction become [bold]STALE[/bold], including captured cards.
A stale card:
- Remains identifiable. - Cannot secure current traffic. - Cannot decode current secured traffic. - Is marked [bold]STALE[/bold] when examined or viewed in the radio panel.
Newly issued cards use the faction's current generation. Replacement cards are obtained through the normal supply system.
## Transmit Power
Select [bold]TX[/bold] to change transmit power.
[bold]Power[/bold]
[bold]Relay range[/bold]
[bold]Battery use[/bold]
[bold]DF signature[/bold]
[bold]LO[/bold]
60% of standard range.
Half.
Half.
[bold]MED[/bold]
Standard range.
Standard.
Standard.
[bold]HI[/bold]
Approximately 150% of standard range.
Double.
Double.
## Direction Finding
Enemy electronic-warfare equipment can locate transmissions that create a direction-finding signature.
A signature can be produced by:
- PT mode. - Faction-net traffic sent without matching fill. - FH mode, including secured FH traffic. - Higher transmit power. - Repeated transmissions from the same location. - Transmitting near enemy jamming.
Secured SC and CT traffic do not create a direction-finding signature.
## Radio Check
Select [bold]RADIO CHECK[/bold] to test coverage on the active net.
The radio transmits:
[bold]RADIO CHECK, HOW COPY, OVER[/bold]
The operator receives a private report listing stations in clear or degraded coverage.
When performed inside enemy jamming, the report also includes a bearing to the strongest jammer.
## Retransmission Stations
A trained operator can deploy a radio with [bold]Set Up Retrans[/bold].
A retransmission station:
- Relays its loaded faction nets. - Continues recording traffic. - Consumes battery power. - Retains its presets. - Retains its fill card. - Allows nearby users to take its handset.
Any user can select [bold]Pack Up Radio[/bold], including hostile personnel.
A captured retransmission station can therefore provide access to its radio, handset, and loaded fill card.
## Fixed Infrastructure
Powered shipboard [bold]communications arrays[/bold] anchor faction nets across the ship, including every deck.
Command-post [bold]antenna masts[/bold] provide local coverage without electrical power. Masts, worn radios, and retransmission stations reach one level above or below their own; only an array covers a whole site vertically.
Combat nets require coverage from at least one of the following:
- A worn AN/PRC. - A deployed retransmission station. - A communications array. - A command-post antenna mast.
If all available relays are disabled or removed, the affected net loses coverage.
## Battery and Antenna
The radio uses a battery in its internal battery slot.
Power is consumed:
- Slowly while the radio is idle. - More quickly while transmitting. - More quickly while performing radio checks. - According to the selected operating mode and transmit power.
The standard [bold]whip antenna[/bold] supports mobile operation.
A [bold]mast antenna[/bold]:
- Provides greater range while stationary. - Provides less range than the whip while moving. - Is intended for command posts and retransmission sites.
## Intercepting Enemy Traffic
Enter another faction's frequency with [bold]FREQ[/bold].
When the signal reaches the radio, intercepted traffic appears in [color=#FF6B6B]red[/color] and is recorded in the net log.
What can be received depends on the transmitting radio's mode and COMSEC state.
[bold]Traffic[/bold]
[bold]Without matching fill[/bold]
Headset traffic
Received as static.
Secured AN/PRC traffic
Received as static.
Unfilled AN/PRC traffic
Readable.
PT traffic
Readable.
FH traffic
Readable when sent in the clear, with light interference.
CT traffic
Not displayed.
A current fill card from the transmitting faction allows compatible secured traffic to be read until that faction performs recrypto.
Intercepted traffic is identified by callsign. It does not reveal the sender's character name or role.
The radio relays only its own faction's nets. Tuning another faction's frequency allows reception, but does not extend that faction's coverage.
## Net Log
The radio stores the last [bold]50[/bold] entries while powered.
The log records:
- Transmissions sent with [color=#a4885c]:r[/color]. - The wearer's headset transmissions. - Incoming traffic on the active net. - Incoming traffic on all loaded nets while MON or SCAN is enabled.
The log can be used to review missed traffic or confirm that a message was transmitted.
## Status Display
The top of the radio panel shows its current status.
- [bold]PWR[/bold] shows the power state. - [bold]LINK[/bold] shows the current link state. - [bold]MODE[/bold] shows the selected operating mode. - [bold]BIT[/bold] shows built-in test status. - [bold]FAULT[/bold] shows detected problems.
[bold]READY[/bold] means the radio is worn and has an active net.
[bold]NO NET[/bold], [bold]STBY[/bold], and [bold]NOT WORN[/bold] indicate that part of the setup is incomplete.
